system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

{"status": "success", "output": "=== cocodrilo-fitness/Dockerfile ===
FROM node:20-alpine AS base
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV PORT=3000
COPY --from=base /app/public ./public
COPY --from=base /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=base /app/.next/static ./.next/static
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

=== cocodrilo-fitness/docker-compose.yml ===
version: "3.8"

services:
  web:
    build: .
    image: cocodrilo-fitness:prod
    ports:
      - "3000"
    networks:
      - agent-net
    deploy:
      mode: replicated
      replicas: 2
      labels:
        - "traefik.enable=true"
        - "traefik.http.routers.cocodrilo-fitness.rule=Host(`cocodrilo-fitness.paragu-ai.com`)"
        - "traefik.http.routers.cocodrilo-fitness.entrypoints=websecure"
        - "traefik.http.routers.cocodrilo-fitness.tls=true"
        - "traefik.http.routers.cocodrilo-fitness.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver"
        - "traefik.http.services.cocodrilo-fitness.loadbalancer.server.port=3000"

networks:
  agent-net:
    external: true

    r = terminal(f"docker service logs {svc_id} --tail 20 --no-trunc 2>&1", timeout=15)
    print(f"=== {name} ===")
    print(r["output"])
    print()
"}

All 5 services have the same critical error: **`Error: Cannot find module '/app/server.js'`** — the Dockerfile uses `standalone` output and CMD `node server.js`, but the repo was built with `npm ci` from code that was pushed as source, not as a pre-built standalone artifact.

Wait — I see some containers run successfully ("✓ Ready in 0ms") and others crash with "Cannot find module '/app/server.js'". The images need to be rebuilt from the actual repo code. Let me check what's actually in the built images vs the repos.

The Docker workflow:
1. Build stage: `npm ci && npm run build` — generates `.next/standalone/`
2. Runner stage: copies `.next/standalone` which should include `server.js`

The error means the images were built from code that produces a differently named output, OR the images were built from an old commit that didn't have `output: "standalone"` yet. The repos now have `output: "standalone"` in next.config.ts, so they need a rebuild.

Let me rebuild all 5.

function.name: execute_code
function.arguments:
{"code": "from hermes_tools import terminal

# First check if the new repos have the standalone output properly
for name in ["cocodrilo-fitness", "luis-de-leon-concept", "bichos-gym", "mantra-spa", "magnolia-peluqueria"]:
    r = terminal(f"cat /root/{name}/next.config.ts", workdir="/root")
    print(f"{name}: {r['output'].strip()}")
    
    # Check package.json has "output: standalone" in it
    r2 = terminal(f"grep -c 'standalone' /root/{name}/.next/next.config.js 2>/dev/null || echo 'no .next dir in repo'", workdir="/root")
    print(f"  .next dir: {r2['output'].strip()}")
"}

messages.content:
Good — the repos have `output: "standalone"` but the Docker images were built from an older version. Need to rebuild all 5 images and redeploy.

With 5 builds each taking ~2-3 min, this'll take a while. Let me run them in parallel batches.

{"code": "from hermes_tools import terminal

stacks = [
    "cocodrilo-fitness",
    "luis-de-leon-concept",
    "bichos-gym",
    "mantra-spa",
    "magnolia-peluqueria",
]

for stack in stacks:
    r = terminal(f"docker stack deploy -c /root/{stack}/docker-compose.yml {stack} 2>&1", timeout=60)
    print(f"{stack}: {r['output'].strip()}")
"}

{"code": "from hermes_tools import terminal
import time

tests = [
    ("cocodrilo-fitness", 30027),
    ("luis-de-leon-concept", 30026),
    ("bichos-gym", 30025),
    ("mantra-spa", 30024),
    ("magnolia-peluqueria", 30023),
]

# Wait a bit more for containers to fully start
time.sleep(5)

for name, port in tests:
    r = terminal(f"curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{{http_code}}' --max-time 10 http://localhost:{port}/ 2>&1", timeout=15)
    status = r['output']
    if status == "200":
        # Also check the static assets work
        r2 = terminal(f"curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{{http_code}}' --max-time 10 http://localhost:{port}/_next/static/chunks/ 2>&1 || true", timeout=15)
        print(f"{name} (:{port}): {status}")
    else:
        print(f"{name} (:{port}): {status}")
"}

function.name: terminal
function.arguments: {"command": "docker logs $(docker ps --filter name=cocodrilo-fitness_web.1 -q --latest) --tail 20 2>&1", "timeout": 15}

messages.content:
{"output": "▲ Next.js 16.2.4
- Local:         http://5f71daac8b39:3000
- Network:       http://5f71daac8b39:3000
✓ Ready in 0ms", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

Server says "Ready" but hangs on requests. This is likely because the standalone server is listening on `http://5f71daac8b39:3000` (the container hostname) but not on `0.0.0.0`. The Dockerfile's `ENV PORT=3000` and `CMD ["node", "server.js"]` works, but Next.js standalone expects `HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0` to listen on all interfaces.

function.name: execute_code
function.arguments:
{"code": "from hermes_tools import terminal, patch

# Fix the Dockerfile for all 5 repos — add HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0 env
repos = [
    "cocodrilo-fitness", "luis-de-leon-concept", "bichos-gym",
    "mantra-spa", "magnolia-peluqueria"
]

for r in repos:
    # Check current Dockerfile
    res = terminal(f"grep 'HOSTNAME\\|host' /root/{r}/Dockerfile 2>&1", workdir="/root")
    print(f"{r}: {res['output']}")
"}

{"status": "success", "output": "cocodrilo-fitness: 
luis-de-leon-concept: 
bichos-gym: 
mantra-spa: 
magnolia-peluqueria: 
", "tool_calls_made": 5, "duration_seconds": 1.64}
No HOSTNAME set. That's the bug. Let me fix all 5 Dockerfiles.

function.name: execute_code
function.arguments:
{"code": "from hermes_tools import patch

repos = [
    "cocodrilo-fitness", "luis-de-leon-concept", "bichos-gym",
    "mantra-spa", "magnolia-peluqueria"
]

for r in repos:
    path = f"/root/{r}/Dockerfile"
    patch(
        path=path,
        old_string="ENV PORT=3000",
        new_string="ENV PORT=3000\nENV HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0",
    )
    print(f"Patched {r}")
"}

messages.content:
{"output": "total 36\ndrwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 May 1 21:44 .\ndrwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 May 1 21:45 ..\ndrwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 May 1 21:45 .next\ndrwxr-xr-x 12 root root 4096 May 1 21:44 node_modules\n-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 570 May 1 21:44 package.json\ndrwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 May 1 19:53 public\n-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 6757 May 1 21:44 server.js", "exit_code": 0, "error": null} [Subdirectory context discovered: /app/AGENTS.md] # Paragu-AI Builder - AI Agent Configuration > AI-first development configuration for the Universal Website Generation Engine > This file guides AI agents working on this project ## Project Context **Paragu-AI Builder** is a multi-tenant, multi-locale website generation engine for Paraguayan businesses. It uses: - **Next.js 15** + **TypeScript** + **Tailwind 3.4.19** - **Supabase** (PostgreSQL + Auth) - **Cloudflare Pages** (deployment) - **Token-based theming** (CSS variables, not hardcoded colors) - **21 reusable section components** serving 20+ business types ## AI Agent Quick Start ### When Starting Work on This Project 1. **Read This File** - You are here! ✅ 2. **Read CLAUDE.md** - Technical architecture and coding standards 3. **Check Current State** - Run `git status` to see what's happening 4. **Check Open PRs** - Run `gh pr list` to see in-flight work 5. **Review Recent Commits** - Run `git log --oneline -10` ### Critical Rules (NEVER Break These) ``` ⚠️ DO NOT upgrade Tailwind to v4 (breaks build) ⚠️ DO NOT hardcode colors - always use var(--primary) ⚠️ DO NOT skip business_id in database queries ⚠️ DO NOT commit .env files ⚠️ DO NOT silently catch errors - always log/rethrow ``` ## Project Structure for AI Agents ``` src/ # Data layer (JSON configs) ├── schemas/ # Business type schemas ├── tokens/ # Design tokens (colors, fonts) ├── registry/ # Business type registry └── content/ # Content templates web/ # Application layer ├── app/ │ ├── [business]/ # Generated sites (SSG) │ ├── admin/ # Admin dashboard │ ├── api/ # API routes │ └── s/[locale]/ # Multi-tenant sites ├── components/ │ ├── sections/ # 21 reusable sections │ └── ui/ # UI primitives (CVA-based) ├── lib/ │ ├── supabase/ # Database clients │ ├── engine/ # Template engine │ └── types.ts # TypeScript types └── scripts/ # Build/generation scripts ``` ## AI-Specific Patterns ### 1. Adding New Business Type (Most Common Task) When adding a new business type (e.g., `dentist`, `mechanic`): **Required Files:** ``` src/ ├── registry/[type].type.json # Section config, features ├── tokens/[type].tokens.json # Colors, fonts ├── content/[type].content.json # Spanish copy templates └── schemas/[type].schema.json # Validation schema ``` **Reuses:** - ✅ Existing section components from `web/components/sections/` - ✅ Base tokens from `src/tokens/base.tokens.json` - ✅ No new React components needed (usually) **Example (5-minute add):** ```json // src/registry/dentist.type.json { "id": "dentist", "nameEs": "Consultorio Dental", "tokens": "dentist", "sections": ["hero", "services", "team", "testimonials", "contact"], "features": { "booking": true, "emergency": true } } ``` ### 2. Adding New Section Component When adding a new reusable section: **File:** `web/components/sections/[name]-section.tsx` **Pattern:** ```typescript interface [Name]SectionProps { title: string items: Array<{...}> // Theme automatically applied via CSS variables } export function [Name]Section({ title, items }: [Name]SectionProps) { return (

{title}

{/* Content */}
) } ``` **Registration:** Add to `web/lib/engine/renderer.tsx` ### 3. Database Schema Changes When modifying Supabase schema: **Location:** `supabase/migrations/000_complete_schema.sql` **Rules:** - Add new tables at the end - Include RLS policies for every table - Add indexes for query performance - Create views for analytics if needed - Test with `npm run typecheck` ### 4. Environment Variables **Local:** `web/.env.local` (gitignored) **Production:** Cloudflare Pages dashboard **Required:** ``` NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL= NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY= SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY= ``` **Optional:** ``` MERCADOPAGO_PUBLIC_KEY= MERCADOPAGO_ACCESS_TOKEN= NEXT_PUBLIC_GA_MEASUREMENT_ID= ``` ## AI-Optimized Workflows ### Workflow 1: Generate Preview Site for Lead **Context:** Admin wants to generate a demo site for a lead **Steps:** 1. Query lead from `leads` table 2. Compose page using `composePage()` from business type 3. Generate static HTML 4. Store in `generated_sites` table 5. Return preview URL **Key Files:** - `web/lib/engine/compose.ts` - Page composition - `web/lib/engine/renderer.tsx` - Section rendering - `web/app/admin/leads/page.tsx` - Admin UI ### Workflow 2: WhatsApp Outreach **Context:** Send templated WhatsApp message to lead **Steps:** 1. Get lead phone/whatsapp from `leads` table 2. Generate wa.me link with templated message 3. Log event to `outreach_events` table 4. Update lead status to `contacted` **Template Location:** `web/lib/outreach/templates.ts` (create if needed) ### Workflow 3: MercadoPago Payment **Context:** Lead wants to subscribe **Steps:** 1. Create subscription in `subscriptions` table 2. Generate MercadoPago checkout 3. Handle webhook at `/api/webhooks/mercadopago` 4. Update subscription status on payment 5. Grant access to self-service editing ## Common AI Tasks & Solutions ### Task: Fix Build Error **Checklist:** 1. `npm run typecheck` - TypeScript errors 2. `npm run lint` - ESLint errors 3. Check for Tailwind v4 (should be 3.4.19) 4. Check for hardcoded colors (should use CSS vars) 5. Check for missing `business_id` in queries ### Task: Debug Database Connection **Checklist:** 1. `.env.local` exists with correct keys 2. Supabase project is running 3. RLS policies allow the operation 4. Using correct client (server vs browser vs admin) ### Task: Add New Feature **Questions to Ask:** 1. Can this reuse existing sections? (95% of cases: YES) 2. Does it need new database tables? (check schema first) 3. Is it admin-only or public? (affects auth) 4. Does it need MercadoPago? (payments feature) ### Task: Optimize Performance **Strategies:** 1. Use Server Components by default 2. Lazy load heavy components 3. Add database indexes for common queries 4. Use Cloudflare caching rules 5. Minimize client-side JavaScript ## AI Tools Available ### Firecrawl Skills (Web Scraping) Installed and ready to use: ```typescript // Use skill for web research import { skill } from '@/lib/skills' // Example: Research competitor websites await skill('firecrawl-scrape', { url: 'https://competitor.com', format: 'markdown' }) ``` **Available Skills:** - `firecrawl-scrape` - Single page extraction - `firecrawl-crawl` - Site-wide crawling - `firecrawl-search` - Web search with content - `firecrawl-map` - URL discovery - `firecrawl-agent` - Structured data extraction - `firecrawl-download` - Bulk download ### When to Use Skills ✅ Research competitor pricing ✅ Extract content from existing business websites ✅ Find images/logos for leads ✅ Validate business information ❌ Don't use for: Database queries, template rendering, auth operations ## Testing for AI Agents ### Quick Test Commands ```bash # Type checking npm run typecheck # Build test npm run build # Unit tests npm run test:unit # E2E tests (requires dev server) npm run dev & npm run test:e2e ``` ### What to Test **New Business Type:** - Registry file is valid JSON - Tokens file has all required fields - Content template renders correctly - Section order works in compose **New Section:** - Renders without errors - Accepts all props - Uses CSS variables (no hardcoded colors) - Responsive on mobile **Database Changes:** - Migration runs without errors - RLS policies work - Indexes improve query speed ## AI Communication Guidelines ### Commit Messages Use conventional commits: ``` feat: Add dentist business type fix: Resolve token resolution for missing palettes refactor: Simplify hostname mapping docs: Upda [...truncated AGENTS.md: 9,940 chars total]

The server works perfectly **inside the container**. But `localhost:30027` hangs. The issue is **Docker Swarm ingress routing mesh**. The port is published to the host but the routing mesh isn't forwarding properly. Let me check the iptables/Docker proxy.

function.name: terminal
function.arguments: {"command": "# Check if the Docker proxy is listening properly\niptables -t nat -L DOCKER -n 2>&1 | grep -E '30027|3000'", "timeout": 10}

messages.content: {"output": "DNAT       [REDACTED_PHONE].0.0.0/[REDACTED_PHONE].0.0.0/0            tcp dpt:3001 to:172.20.0.3:3000", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}
There it is — iptables DNAT rule goes to port 3001 on the wrong network. Let me check all the DNAT rules:

function.name: terminal
function.arguments: {"command": "iptables -t nat -L DOCKER -n 2>&1 | grep -E 'tcp dpt:300'", "timeout": 10}

messages.content: {"output": "DNAT       [REDACTED_PHONE].0.0.0/[REDACTED_PHONE].0.0.0/0            tcp dpt:3001 to:172.20.0.3:3000", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

The only DNAT rule is for port 3001 → 172.20.0.3. The Docker Swarm port mappings for [REDACTED_PHONE] aren't in iptables. But the ports show as LISTEN by dockerd earlier... Let me check if the routing mesh uses IPVS instead.

function.name: terminal

messages.content: {"output": "/usr/bin/bash: line 3: ipvsadm: command not found", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

The container has two networks: `agent-net` (10.0.2.226) and `ingress` (10.0.0.69). The ingress network is used for the Swarm routing mesh. Let me check the ingress network gateway.

function.name: terminal
function.arguments: {"command": "docker network inspect ingress 2>&1 | grep -E '\"Gateway|\"Subnet\"|IPAM' | head -10", "timeout": 10}

messages.content: Ingress gateway is 10.0.0.1. Let me test connectivity more directly.

function.name: terminal
function.arguments: {"command": "# Hit the ingress VIP directly\ncurl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 5 http://10.0.0.1:30027/ 2>&1", "timeout": 10}

messages.content: {"output": "000", "exit_code": 28, "error": null, "approval": "Command required approval (Security scan — [MEDIUM] URL uses raw IP address: URL points to IP address 10.0.0.1 instead of a domain name; [HIGH] Plain HTTP URL in execution context: URL 'http://10.0.0.1:30027/' uses unencrypted HTTP and is being passed to a command that downloads or executes content. An attacker on the network could modify the content.; [HIGH] Private network access: 10.0.0.1: Command accesses private network address 10.0.0.1, which may indicate SSRF or lateral movement) and was approved by the user."}

Let me check the iptables rules for the DOCKER-INGRESS chain specifically:

function.name: terminal
function.arguments: {"command": "iptables -t nat -L DOCKER-INGRESS -n -v 2>&1", "timeout": 10}

messages.content: There's the issue! The DNAT rule forwards 30027 to **172.27.0.2:30027**, but the container is on 10.0.0.69 (ingress). The target 172.27.0.2 is the docker_gwbridge. This DNAT rule is correct for Swarm — it routes to the ingress VIP 172.27.0.2. Let me check if there's something at 172.27.0.2:30027.

function.name: terminal
function.arguments: {"command": "# Try connecting to the ingress VIP\ncurl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 5 http://172.27.0.2:30027/ 2>&1", "timeout": 10}

messages.content: {"output": "200", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}
**IT WORKS!** 172.27.0.2:30027 returns 200. The routing mesh is functional — the localhost bind must have a different path. Let me verify the other sites too, then check why localhost fails.

function.name: terminal
function.arguments: {"command": "for p in [REDACTED_PHONE]; do echo -n \":$p => \"; curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 5 http://172.27.0.2:$p/ 2>&1; echo \"\"; done", "timeout": 30}

messages.content: **ALL 5 SITES RETURN 200!** Now let me verify they're actually accessible from the public URLs.

Found it! **missing `traefik.docker.network=agent-net`** on all 5 broken sites. This label tells Traefik which Docker network to use to reach the service. Without it, Traefik can't route to the containers.

Now I need to fix all 5 docker-compose files to add this label, rebuild, and redeploy.

function.name: execute_code
function.arguments:
{"code": "from hermes_tools import patch, terminal

repos = [
    "cocodrilo-fitness", "luis-de-leon-concept", "bichos-gym",
    "mantra-spa", "magnolia-peluqueria"
]

for r in repos:
    path = f"/root/{r}/docker-compose.yml"
    old = '        - "traefik.enable=true"'
    new = '        - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net"\n        - "traefik.enable=true"'
    res = patch(path=path, old_string=old, new_string=new)
    print(f"{r}: {'patched' if 'Success' in str(res) else res}")

# Verify all have the fix
for r in repos:
    res = terminal(f"grep 'traefik.docker.network' /root/{r}/docker-compose.yml 2>&1", workdir="/root")
    print(f"{r}: {res['output']}")
"}

messages.content: All 5 docker-compose files patched. Now redeploy the stacks to update the labels.

Done. Summary of findings and fixes:

**Root Cause — 2 issues in all 5 auto-generated sites:**

1. **`Dockerfile` missing `ENV HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0`** — Next.js standalone server was listening on container hostname (e.g. `http://ad664f58d66b:3000`) instead of `0.0.0.0:3000`. This made the container unreachable via the Docker Swarm routing mesh.

2. **`docker-compose.yml` missing `traefik.docker.network=agent-net`** label — Without this, Traefik couldn't find the correct Docker overlay network to route HTTP requests to the containers. Working sites (nexa, fun4me, superspuma) all have this label.

**Fixes applied:**
- Added `ENV HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0` to all 5 Dockerfiles
- Added `traefik.docker.network=agent-net` to all 5 docker-compose.yml deploy labels
- Rebuilt all Docker images with the fixes
- Redeployed all 5 Swarm stacks
- Pushed fixes to GitHub (Ai-Whisperers org)

**Results:**
- https://cocodrilo-fitness.paragu-ai.com → 200 ✓
- https://luis-de-leon-concept.paragu-ai.com → 200 ✓
- https://bichos-gym.paragu-ai.com → 200 ✓
- https://mantra-spa.paragu-ai.com → 200 ✓
- https://magnolia-peluqueria.paragu-ai.com → 200 ✓

**Other working sites checked:** nexa, fun4me, superspuma all return 200. dayah-litworks and elviajero show 000 but the Docker services are running — those are DNS record issues (no Cloudflare A records for those subdomains).

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

version: 1.0.0

# VPS Hardening — Docker Swarm + Compose Hybrid

## When to Use
- Setting up or hardening a single-node Docker Swarm VPS
- Adding new services behind Traefik reverse proxy
- After Docker restart/upgrade (services need reconnection)
- Setting up automated backups for Docker infrastructure

## Phase 1: Security Hardening

### 1a. UFW Firewall
CRITICAL: Allow SSH FIRST before enabling, or you get locked out.

```bash
ufw default deny incoming
ufw default allow outgoing
ufw allow 22/tcp comment 'SSH'
ufw allow 80/tcp comment 'HTTP'
ufw allow 443/tcp comment 'HTTPS'
# Docker internal ranges
ufw allow from 172.16.0.0/12 comment 'Docker internal'
ufw allow from 10.0.0.0/8 comment 'Docker overlay'
ufw allow from 192.168.0.0/16 comment 'Docker bridge'
# Swarm ports
ufw allow 2377/tcp comment 'Swarm management'
ufw allow 7946/tcp comment 'Swarm node comm'
ufw allow 7946/udp comment 'Swarm node comm'
ufw allow 4789/udp comment 'Swarm overlay'
echo 'y' | ufw enable
```

This automatically blocks any services on non-standard ports (like Qdrant 6333, LiteLLM 4000) from external access while keeping them reachable via Docker networks.

### 1b. Swap Space
Even with 32GB RAM, add swap as an OOM safety net:
```bash
fallocate -l 4G /swapfile
chmod 600 /swapfile
mkswap /swapfile
swapon /swapfile
echo '/swapfile none swap sw 0 0' >> /etc/fstab
sysctl vm.swappiness=10
echo 'vm.swappiness=10' >> /etc/sysctl.d/99-swap.conf
```

### 1c. Docker daemon.json

**PITFALL: `live-restore: true` is INCOMPATIBLE with Docker Swarm mode.**
Docker will fail to start with: `failed to start cluster component: --live-restore daemon configuration is incompatible with swarm mode`

Correct config for Swarm:
```json
{
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "10m",
    "max-file": "3"
  },
  "default-ulimits": {
    "nofile": {"Name": "nofile", "Hard": 65536, "Soft": 65536}
  },
  "storage-driver": "overlay2",
  "metrics-addr": "127.0.0.1:9323"
}
```

After writing to `/etc/docker/daemon.json`:
```bash
systemctl restart docker
```

**PITFALL: Docker restart on Swarm loses locally-built images.**
Swarm services using local images (built with `docker build`, not pulled from registry) will go to 0/1 replicas with "No such image" error. Must rebuild them after restart:
```bash
cd /path/to/project && docker build -t image-name:latest .
# Swarm will automatically pick up the new image
```

## Phase 2: Traefik + Swarm/Compose Hybrid Networking

### The Core Problem
Traefik runs as a Swarm service on an overlay network (`agent-net`). Compose projects CANNOT attach to Swarm overlay networks (`rpc error: network agent-net not manually attachable`).

### Solution: Bridge Networks + Traefik Reconnection
Each Compose project uses its own bridge network. Traefik connects to each bridge network to discover and route to them.

Compose project pattern:
```yaml
services:
  myapp:
    image: myapp:latest
    labels:
      - "traefik.enable=true"
      - "traefik.docker.network=myapp-net"   # CRITICAL - see below
      - "traefik.http.routers.myapp.rule=Host(`myapp.example.com`)"
      - "traefik.http.routers.myapp.entrypoints=websecure"
      - "traefik.http.routers.myapp.tls=true"
      - "traefik.http.routers.myapp.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver"
      - "traefik.http.services.myapp.loadbalancer.server.port=3000"
    networks:
      - myapp-net

networks:
  myapp-net:
    name: myapp-net
```

**CRITICAL: `traefik.docker.network` label is REQUIRED** when Traefik has `--providers.docker.network=agent-net` (Swarm default). Without it, Traefik tries to route through agent-net overlay and gets 404/502. The label tells Traefik which bridge network to use to reach the Compose container. This was the root cause of new services returning 404 — Traefik discovered them but couldn't route to them.

**PITFALL: Labels must be at service level (not under `deploy:`) for Compose projects.** Swarm services use `deploy.labels`, Compose services use service-level `labels`.

**PITFALL: `docker service update --label-add` does NOT persist labels to `TaskTemplate.ContainerSpec`** where Traefik's Docker Swarm provider reads from. The labels go to a different field and Traefik ignores them. Always use `docker stack deploy -c compose.yml` with labels in the compose file.

### Traefik Reconnection Script
After Docker restart, Traefik loses bridge network connections. Create `/opt/scripts/traefik-reconnect.sh`:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
TRAEFIK_ID=$(docker ps -q -f name=traefik_traefik)
[ -z "$TRAEFIK_ID" ] && echo "Traefik not running!" && exit 1

# Re-apply postgres alias on pg-bridge (lost on container restart)
PG_ID=$(docker ps -q -f name=postgres_postgres)
[ -n "$PG_ID" ] && docker network connect --alias postgres pg-bridge "$PG_ID" 2>/dev/null || true

NETWORKS=(myapp-net another-net)  # list all bridge networks
for net in "${NETWORKS[@]}"; do
    docker network connect "$net" "$TRAEFIK_ID" 2>/dev/null && \
        echo "Connected: $net" || echo "Already: $net"
done
```

Automate with cron and systemd:
```bash
# Cron @reboot
@reboot sleep 30 && /opt/scripts/traefik-reconnect.sh >> /opt/backups/traefik-reconnect.log 2>&1

# Docker post-start hook: /etc/systemd/system/docker.service.d/traefik-reconnect.conf
[Service]
ExecStartPost=/bin/bash -c 'sleep 15 && /opt/scripts/traefik-reconnect.sh &'
```

### Compose→Swarm PostgreSQL Access
Compose containers can't reach Swarm services directly. Solution: bridge network with alias.

```bash
docker network create pg-bridge
# Connect Swarm postgres with a stable alias
docker network connect --alias postgres pg-bridge $(docker ps -q -f name=postgres_postgres)
# Connect Compose services
docker network connect pg-bridge myapp
```

In Compose files, add pg-bridge as external network:
```yaml
services:
  myapp:
    networks:
      - myapp-net
      - pg-bridge
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: [REDACTED_DB_URL]  myapp-net:
    name: myapp-net
  pg-bridge:
    external: true
```

**PITFALL: The postgres alias on pg-bridge must be re-applied after Docker restart** (Swarm container gets new ID). Add to reconnect script.

## Phase 3: Automated Backups

Daily backup script (`/opt/backups/backup.sh`):
1. `pg_dumpall` from postgres container → gzipped
2. Docker volume backup via alpine tar container
3. Config files (stack yamls, .env, Hermes config)
4. Retention: 7 days

Cron schedule:
```
0 3 * * * /opt/backups/backup.sh          # PG + volumes + configs
0 4 * * * /opt/backups/qdrant-backup.sh   # Qdrant snapshots
0 5 * * 0 docker system prune -f --filter "until=72h"  # Weekly cleanup
```

## Phase 4: Monitoring Stack (Prometheus + Grafana)

Deploy a monitoring stack for host + Docker metrics:

```yaml
# /opt/monitoring/docker-compose.yml
services:
  prometheus:
    image: prom/prometheus:latest
    volumes:
      - ./prometheus/prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml
      - prometheus_data:/prometheus
    ports:
      - "9090:9090"
    command:
      - "--config.file=/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml"

  grafana:
    image: grafana/grafana:latest
    volumes:
      - grafana_data:/var/lib/grafana
      - ./grafana/provisioning:/etc/grafana/provisioning
    ports:
      - "3001:3000"  # host 3001 to avoid conflicts
    environment:
      - GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD: [REDACTED]  prometheus_data:
  grafana_data:
```

Enable Docker metrics in `/etc/docker/daemon.json`:
```json
{
  "metrics-addr": "0.0.0.0:9323",
  "experimental": true
}
```

Node exporter (Swarm global service):
```bash
docker service create --mode global --name node-exporter \
  --publish mode=host,target=9100 \
  prom/node-exporter:latest
```

Prometheus config (`/opt/monitoring/prometheus/prometheus.yml`):
```yaml
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s

scrape_configs:
  - job_name: "prometheus"
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["localhost:9090"]
  - job_name: "node"
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["node-exporter:9100"]
  - job_name: "docker"
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["host.docker.internal:9323"]
```

**PITFALL: Grafana datasource config** — only one datasource can be `isDefault: true`. If you get "Datasource provisioning error", remove `isDefault: true` from your datasource YAML.

**PITFALL: Port conflicts** — check what's on 3000 first with `ss -tlnp | grep :3000`. Use 3001+ for Grafana if taken.

**PITFALL: Compose `deploy:` block** — in `docker compose` (v2), the `deploy` block is ignored. Use simple `ports:` and `volumes:` instead.

Traefik route for Grafana (`/opt/traefik/dynamic/grafana.yml`):
```yaml
http:
  routers:
    grafana:
      rule: "Host(`metrics.example.com`)"
      entrypoints:
        - websecure
      service: grafana
      tls:
        certresolver: letsencrypt
  services:
    grafana:
      loadBalancer:
        servers:
          - url: "http://127.0.0.1:3001"
```

Recommended additions with Traefik routing:
- **Uptime Kuma** (~100MB) — status.domain.com — monitoring + alerts
- **n8n** (~230MB) — n8n.domain.com — AI workflow automation
- **Open WebUI** (~400MB) — chat.domain.com — LLM chat interface
- **Umami** (~100MB) — analytics.domain.com — privacy analytics

All need DNS A records pointing to the VPS IP.

## Pitfalls Summary

| Problem | Cause | Fix |
|---------|-------|-----|
| Docker won't start after daemon.json | `live-restore: true` + Swarm | Remove `live-restore` from daemon.json |
| Compose can't use Swarm overlay | Overlay networks not attachable | Use bridge networks + Traefik reconnect |
| Swarm service 0/1 after restart | Locally-built image lost | Rebuild: `docker build -t name:latest .` |
| New Compose service unreachable | Traefik not on its network | `docker network connect net traefik-id` |
| Compose can't reach Swarm DB | Different network namespace | pg-bridge with `--alias postgres` |
| Labels ignored in Compose | Labels under `deploy:` | Move labels to service level |
| Umami/n8n can't auth to postgres | User/DB not created | Create DB+user in postgres BEFORE starting |
| Docker prune removes active images | Swarm images are "unused" during restart | Prune AFTER all services are stable |
| fun4me-style: stale network label | Old network exists with wrong labels | `docker network rm` then recreate |
| Stack deploy ignores build: in compose | Must build image separately first | `docker build ...` THEN `docker stack deploy` |
| Stale Traefik router after stack rename | Old service router lingers | `docker stack rm` old stack fully before redeploying |
| Cf API token has no DNS write | Token scope = zone:read only | Add DNS A records manually in Cloudflare dashboard |
| LE rate limited on new subdomain | DNS didn't exist during first LE attempt | Wait 1 hour, DNS must resolve before Traefik sees it |
| Port 3000 conflict on restart | Multiple services bind same host port | Remove `ports:` from Compose — let Traefik handle routing |
| 404 with Traefik + Compose | Missing `traefik.docker.network` label | Add label pointing to the service's bridge network |
| pg-bridge alias lost | Swarm PG container restarted (new ID) | Re-run `docker network connect --alias postgres pg-bridge` |
| UFW locks you out | Enabled before allowing SSH | ALWAYS `ufw allow 22/tcp` first |
| **Shell broken after deleting CWD** | Ran `rm -rf` on dir the shell was `cd`'d into | Use `background=true` flag for destructive ops on the shell's CWD; or `cd /` first |
| **`--label-add` doesn't persist for Traefik** | Labels go to `ContainerSpec`, Traefik reads `TaskTemplate` | Use `docker stack deploy -c compose.yml` with labels in file |
| **nginx listening on 8080, never receiving traffic** | Cloudflare origin → VPS:443 → Traefik, bypassing nginx entirely | Check `ss -tlnp` for ghost nginx on non-standard ports; if not in any CF origin rule, it's dead weight |
| **Swarm service has no Traefik labels** | Service deployed without compose file | Create compose with Traefik labels → `docker stack deploy -c` |
| **Site returns 000 from curl** | DNS does not resolve — no A record in Cloudflare | Add `domain.com A VPS_IP` in Cloudflare DNS dashboard |
| **LE fails: DNS not propagated** | Just-added A record not resolved globally | Wait up to 1 hour; Traefik retries every ~15s |
| **File write didn't persist** | `cat >` heredoc in a piped/batched shell command | Use `write_file` tool for critical files |
| **fun4me: 136MB node_modules in git history** | node_modules or `.next` was committed | `git checkout --orphan clean-main` to create fresh history, then force-push |
| **Alejandro repo: empty, no DNS, no content** | Stale scaffold with no actual site | Delete Docker service + GH repo. Tokens with `admin:org` but no `delete_repo` scope need manual deletion. |
| **Clinica: old container on bridge, not in Traefik** | Was deployed via `docker compose` not `docker stack` | Stop old container, copy app into monorepo `apps/`, standardize Dockerfile/compose/Traefik labels, deploy via stack |
| **@ai-whisperers packages not resolvable during Docker build** | `.npmrc` with GH token not in build context | For now, don't add @ai-whisperers deps until packages are public or Docker build can mount the token |
| **Site returns 404 from standalone Next.js catch-all** | `[...slug]` route needs a slug; root `/` is not handled | Access via PAB path or add a root handler in the app |
| **Site returns 526 (Invalid SSL cert) from Cloudflare** | New proxied subdomain — CF defaults to Full (Strict) mode, but origin has self-signed cert | **Sequence:** (1) Create self-signed cert on Traefik via file provider; (2) Change CF SSL to Full (not Strict) in dashboard. Cannot use LE ACME (CF proxy blocks HTTP-01) or CF Origin CA API (token lacks write). See `references/cloudflare-526-ssl-fix.md` for full sequence and pitfalls. |
| **nginx listening on 8080, never receiving traffic** | Cloudflare origin → VPS:443 → Traefik, bypassing nginx:8080 entirely | Check `ss -tlnp` for ghost nginx on non-standard ports; if not in any CF origin rule, it's dead weight |
| **Swarm Docker service has no Traefik labels** | Service deployed without compose file | `docker service ls` shows X/X replicas but `docker service inspect` has empty labels | Create a compose file with Traefik labels and `docker stack deploy -c` |
| **Site returns 000 from curl** | DNS does not resolve — no A record in Cloudflare | Add `domain.com A VPS_IP` in Cloudflare DNS dashboard; services running but unreachable |
| **LE fails: DNS not propagated** | Just-added A record not resolved globally | Wait up to 1 hour; Traefik retries every ~15s |

## Appendix: GitHub Packages Publishing on this VPS

### Key constraint: scope must match org name
When publishing to GitHub Packages, the npm scope MUST match the GitHub org name exactly (case-insensitive lookup). For `Ai-Whisperers` org, the scope is `@ai-whisperers`.

```bash
# Setup .npmrc
echo "//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=$(gh auth token)" > .npmrc
echo "@ai-whisperers:registry=https://npm.pkg.github.com/" >> .npmrc

# Publish (--userconfig required to read the file)
npm publish --userconfig .npmrc

# Token must have write:packages scope
# Check: gh auth status --show-token | grep write:packages
```

### Common failures
- **401 "unauthenticated"** — `.npmrc` not found or `_authToken` env var not expanded. Write inline token, not `$GH_TOKEN`.
- **403 "owner not found"** — Scope doesn't match org name. `@paragu-ai` fails on `Ai-Whisperers` org. Use `@ai-whisperers`.
- **403 "forbidden by security policy"** — Token has `repo` and `write:packages` but scope is wrong.
- **409 "Cannot publish over existing version"** — Bump version in `package.json` and retry.

## Appendix: Hermes Cron Job Management on Swarm VPS

When managing 23+ cron jobs on a VPS running 6+ Docker services, use this audit pattern:

### Cron Job Health Check
```bash
hermes cron list
```
Check `last_status` and `last_delivery_error` for every job. Common failures:
- **`deliver: "whatsapp"` without target ID** — errors with "no delivery target resolved". Fix: set `deliver: "local"` or `deliver: "whatsapp:username"`.
- **`model: null` / `provider: null`** — inherits current session model (cheap). Fix: explicitly set `model: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4"` and `provider: "openrouter"`.
- **Missing `enabled_toolsets`** — skill has tools but job can't use them. Fix: add `["terminal", "file", "skills"]`.

### Automated Worker Pattern (1000-item backlog)
For large autonomous build backlogs, use self-scheduling cron jobs:
1. Create a job that runs every 20m
2. Each execution picks ONE item from a markdown backlog, implements it, marks it `[✅]`
3. Verifies the result with curl/http check
4. Self-schedules continuation (the job's `repeat: forever` handles this — no explicit rescheduling needed)
5. The backlog IS the state — no database, no progress tracking

**Key constraints for autonomous workers:**
- NEVER run without explicit `model` + `provider` (inherits cheap model)
- NEVER use `deliver: "whatsapp"` without a specific target
- Always include `enabled_toolsets` matching the required skill
- Use `skill` parameter to load the correct skill module
- Workers need `terminal` + `file` at minimum; `skills` if they load a skill

### Jobs to Remove
Any job that:
- Never ran (`last_run_at: null`)
- Errors consistently with no fix path
- Was created as a test/experiment with no real purpose
- Has no model/provider set

### Deliver Targets Audit
Correct targets for this VPS:
- `local` — saves to log, no external delivery
- `whatsapp:weissvanderpol` — DM to user (lead-scout only)
- `telegram:Fun4me-IA` — group delivery

NEVER use bare `whatsapp` or bare `telegram` — they error.

## Quick Diagnostic: "Site running but returning 000"

When `curl -so /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://domain.com` returns `000`:

1. **DNS check**: `dig +short domain.com` — empty means no A record in Cloudflare
2. **Docker check**: `docker service ls | grep service_name` — X/X replicas means it's running
3. **Traefik route check**: `docker service inspect svc_name --format '{{range $k,$v := .Spec.TaskTemplate.ContainerSpec.Labels}}{{$k}}={{$v}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}' | grep traefik` — empty labels = no route
4. **Fix DNS**: Add A record manually in Cloudflare dashboard (CF token is read-only)
5. **Fix labels**: Create compose file with labels → `docker stack deploy -c compose.yml`

Deploying per-client tenant sites as standalone Docker Swarm services behind Traefik.
All services on a single `agent-net` overlay network (no Compose bridge hacks).

## Standard Stack Template

```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  web:
    build: .
    image: tenant-name:prod
    ports:
      - "3000"
    networks:
      - agent-net
    deploy:
      mode: replicated
      replicas: 2
      labels:
        - "traefik.enable=true"
        - "traefik.http.routers.tenantname.entrypoints=websecure"
        - "traefik.http.routers.tenantname.rule=Host(`tenant.paragu-ai.com`)"
        - "traefik.http.routers.tenantname.tls=true"
        - "traefik.http.routers.tenantname.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver"
        - "traefik.http.services.tenantname.loadbalancer.server.port=3000"
        - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "--no-verbose", "--tries=1", "--spider", "http://127.0.0.1:3000/"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 15s
networks:
  agent-net:
    external: true
```

## Full Deploy Cycle

```bash
# 1. Build image (separate from deploy — compose build: is ignored by stack deploy)
docker build -t tenant-name:prod .

# 2. Deploy stack
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml tenant-stack-name --detach=false

# 3. Verify
curl -sI https://tenant.paragu-ai.com | head -3
```

## Pitfalls

### P1: `docker stack deploy` ignores `build:` in compose
You must `docker build` separately first. Compose's `build:` key is silently ignored. Stack deploy only uses `image:` tag.

### P2: Stack deploy does NOT update service labels from changed compose
If you edit the compose file and re-run `docker stack deploy`, the existing service's labels (including Traefik rules, hostnames) are NOT updated. You must either:
- **Remove the old stack first**: `docker stack rm <name>` then `docker stack deploy`
- **Or update labels directly**: `docker service update --label-add "traefik.http.routers.X.rule=Host(\`new-host\`)" <service>`

### P3: Old stack names leave stale Traefik routers
If you deploy a stack with one name (`elviajero`) then recreate it with a different name (`elviajero-comercio`), the old service's Traefik router stays in Traefik's config. It causes "Router defined multiple times" errors and can trigger LetsEncrypt rate limits if it keeps requesting certs for a now-nonexistent domain.

**Fix:** Always `docker stack rm <old-name>` fully before redeploying with a new name. Wait for containers to drain.

### P4: CF API token scopes cause silent DNS failures
The stored CF API token (`/root/.cloudflare-env`) has `zone:read` but NOT `dns_records:write`. DNS records must be added manually in the Cloudflare dashboard:
- Zone: paragu-ai.com
- Type: A
- Name: subdomain-name (e.g. `dayah`, `el-viajero`)
- Content: 72.61.44.159
- Proxy: Proxied (orange cloud)

### P5: DNS record name must match Traefik Host() rule exactly
If CF record is `el-viajero.paragu-ai.com` but Traefik rule says `Host(\`viajero.paragu-ai.com\`)`, LetsEncrypt will fail with NXDOMAIN and rate-limit the subdomain. Match exactly.

### P6: LetsEncrypt rate limits from failed attempts
If a subdomain gets 5+ failed authorization attempts (e.g. because DNS didn't exist yet), LE blocks for 1 hour. No workaround — wait.

### P7: `docker service update --label-add` with backtick values in shell
When running `docker service update --label-add 'traefik.http.routers.X.rule=Host(\`domain.com\`)'` in bash, backticks are interpreted as command substitution. Use single quotes and escaped backticks, or use `--label-add` with the literal backtick-free syntax and rely on Traefik's parsing. Safer: use `--label-add` without the Host rule change if you need to swap a domain — or just `docker stack rm` + redeploy.

### P8: Port 3000 conflict between NGINX proxy and standalone apps
When both a nginx proxy container (dayah-litworks) and a standalone Next.js app (elviajero-comercio) run on the same Swarm, they both use port 3000 internally. Swarm's VIP load balancer handles this — but if you test with `curl http://127.0.0.1:3000` you'll hit whichever service last claimed the host port. {"action": "edit", "content": "# Pure Swarm Tenant Deployment — Patterns & Pitfalls

Deploying per-client tenant sites as standalone Docker Swarm services behind Traefik.
All services on a single `agent-net` overlay network (no Compose bridge hacks).

## Standard Stack Template

```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  web:
    build: .
    image: tenant-name:prod
    ports:
      - "3000"
    networks:
      - agent-net
    deploy:
      mode: replicated
      replicas: 2
      labels:
        - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net"
        - "traefik.enable=true"
        - "traefik.http.routers.tenantname.entrypoints=websecure"
        - "traefik.http.routers.tenantname.rule=Host(`tenant.paragu-ai.com`)"
        - "traefik.http.routers.tenantname.tls=true"
        - "traefik.http.routers.tenantname.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver"
        - "traefik.http.services.tenantname.loadbalancer.server.port=3000"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "--no-verbose", "--tries=1", "--spider", "http://127.0.0.1:3000/"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 15s
networks:
  agent-net:
    external: true
```

**IMPORTANT:** The `traefik.docker.network=agent-net` label must be FIRST among deploy labels. Without it, Traefik cannot route to the container.

## Dockerfile for Next.js Standalone

Next.js standalone mode requires the host to bind to 0.0.0.0, not the container hostname:

```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine AS base
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0          # REQUIRED for Swarm routing mesh
COPY --from=base /app/public ./public
COPY --from=base /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=base /app/.next/static ./.next/static
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

Without `ENV HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0`, Next.js listens on the container's hostname (e.g. `http://abc123:3000`) instead of `0.0.0.0:3000`. The container will show "✓ Ready" in logs but `curl localhost:PORT` will hang — the routing mesh can't connect.

## Full Deploy Cycle

```bash
# 1. Build image (separate from deploy — compose build: is ignored by stack deploy)
docker build -t tenant-name:prod .

# 2. Deploy stack
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml tenant-stack-name --detach=false

# 3. Verify
curl -sI https://tenant.paragu-ai.com | head -3
```

## Fault Detection: Next.js container "Ready" but unreachable

When a container shows `✓ Ready in 0ms` but `curl http://localhost:PORT` hangs:

```bash
# Check actual listening interface inside container
docker exec <container-id> ss -tlnp | grep 3000
# Expected: 0.0.0.0:3000  (not a hostname IP)
# Broken:   container-hostname:3000

# Test from inside container directly
docker exec <container-id> wget -q -O - http://127.0.0.1:3000/ 2>&1 | head -3
# If this works but external doesn't, it's a HOSTNAME issue

# Check Traefik service labels include required network
docker service inspect <service> --format '{{range $k,$v := .Spec.Labels}}{{$k}}={{$v}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}' | grep -E 'network|enable'
# Must show: traefik.docker.network=agent-net
```

## Pitfalls

### P1: `docker stack deploy` ignores `build:` in compose
You must `docker build` separately first. Compose's `build:` key is silently ignored. Stack deploy only uses `image:` tag.

### P2: Stack deploy does NOT update service labels from changed compose
If you edit the compose file and re-run `docker stack deploy`, the existing service's labels (including Traefik rules, hostnames) are NOT updated. You must either:
- **Remove the old stack first**: `docker stack rm <name>` then `docker stack deploy`
- **Or update labels directly**: `docker service update --label-add "traefik.http.routers.X.rule=Host(\`new-host\`)" <service>`
- **Or force-update**: `docker service update --force <service>` to trigger a rolling restart with new image

### P3: Old stack names leave stale Traefik routers
If you deploy a stack with one name (`elviajero`) then recreate it with a different name (`elviajero-comercio`), the old service's Traefik router stays in Traefik's config. It causes "Router defined multiple times" errors and can trigger LetsEncrypt rate limits if it keeps requesting certs for a now-nonexistent domain.

**Fix:** Always `docker stack rm <old-name>` fully before redeploying with a new name. Wait for containers to drain.

### P4: CF API token scopes cause silent DNS failures
The stored CF API token (`/root/.cloudflare-env`) has `zone:read` but NOT `dns_records:write`. DNS records must be added manually in the Cloudflare dashboard:
- Zone: paragu-ai.com
- Type: A
- Name: subdomain-name (e.g. `dayah`, `el-viajero`)
- Content: 72.61.44.159
- Proxy: Proxied (orange cloud)

### P5: DNS record name must match Traefik Host() rule exactly
If CF record is `el-viajero.paragu-ai.com` but Traefik rule says `Host(\`viajero.paragu-ai.com\`)`, LetsEncrypt will fail with NXDOMAIN and rate-limit the subdomain. Match exactly.

### P6: LetsEncrypt rate limits from failed attempts
If a subdomain gets 5+ failed authorization attempts (e.g. because DNS didn't exist yet), LE blocks for 1 hour. No workaround — wait.

### P7: `docker service update --label-add` with backtick values in shell
When running `docker service update --label-add 'traefik.http.routers.X.rule=Host(\`domain.com\`)'` in bash, backticks are interpreted as command substitution. Use single quotes and escaped backticks, or use `--label-add` with the literal backtick-free syntax and rely on Traefik's parsing. Safer: use `--label-add` without the Host rule change if you need to swap a domain — or just `docker stack rm` + redeploy.

### P8: Port 3000 conflict between NGINX proxy and standalone apps
When both a nginx proxy container (dayah-litworks) and a standalone Next.js app (elviajero-comercio) run on the same Swarm, they both use port 3000 internally. Swarm's VIP load balancer handles this — but if you test with `curl http://127.0.0.1:3000` you'll hit whichever service last claimed the host port. Use `docker run --rm --network agent-net alpine wget -qO- http://service-name:3000` instead for direct per-service testing.

### P9: Force-update required after image rebuild
When you rebuild a Docker image with the same tag (`image:prod`), the Swarm service won't automatically restart. The old containers keep running. Always run:
```bash
docker service update --force <service>
```
after `docker build` + `docker stack deploy` if the compose file didn't change.

### P10: Two replicas means one might be old
With `replicas: 2`, `docker service update --force` rolls one at a time. During the roll, one old (broken) and one new (fixed) replica coexist temporarily. The old one handles some requests. Wait for `2/2` status before testing.

### P11: Image digest warning is safe to ignore
When deploying locally-built images (no registry), Swarm warns:
```
image could not be accessed on a registry to record its digest
```
This is informational — the service works fine. Each node independently resolves the local image tag.
